unit: ring<T> / deque (VecDeque-class pow2 masked head/tail, wraparound over spare capacity, as_slices) — currently planned as a sealed variant sharing the seq kernel
user_route: Copy T (the dominant case): a taught composition card over the floor — fixed `buffer` of pow2 capacity (Copy, fully initialized, so no uninit problem), `head`/`tail` monotone counters, every access at `i & (cap-1)`, with a requires-prologue fact `cap is pow2 && buf.len == cap` so the masked index is a dominated bound; `as_slices` is two ordinary slice views `[head&m .. cap]` and `[0 .. tail&m]`; growth allocates a new zero-initialized buffer and copies the two regions with the machine-core copy forms. Affine T: users CANNOT build the inline ring — wraparound needs typed uninit-slot arithmetic (holes between head and tail inside allocated storage) and hole-leaving take/replace out of a user container, both explicitly absent from the floor (wbuf is bytes-only). The taught route for affine payloads is the pool-handle queue card: values live in sealed pool<T>, the queue is a Copy ring of generational handles; push = pool.insert + handle push, pop = handle pop + pool.take. A two-seq (inbox/outbox flip) queue card exists as a fallback but moves every element twice and has O(n) worst-case pop, so it is documented as amortized-only. Concurrent ring cases (SPSC/MPMC/steal) are already sealed in conc_queue and are out of scope here.
perf_argument: VecDeque's ~1ns amortized push/pop is mechanically: store (or load) at masked index, counter increment, one predictable full/empty compare, amortized grow. The user Copy-ring card compiles to exactly the same instruction shape provided the facts system discharges `(i & (cap-1)) < cap` from the pow2 prologue fact — the mask fact dominates the bound, which is squarely the dominated-bounds pattern the requires-prologue system was built and measured on (1.71x base64). Nothing in the route adds indirection, allocation, or SIMD loss; the grow path adds one zero-init pass over the new buffer on top of VecDeque's realloc-and-two-memcpys, which is amortized noise. So Copy ring hits par from the floor. Affine-inline ring does NOT hit par from the floor: the pool-handle route adds one pointer deref plus a generation compare per element touch and loses element contiguity, roughly matching VecDeque<Box<T>> (boxed workloads: at par or better, since the slab beats per-item malloc) but missing par for hot small-affine-T inline deques. That residual gap is real — but no operation in the frozen fourteen-op Hashbrown/mimalloc/SQLite/Crossbeam corpus is a single-threaded affine deque (Crossbeam's deque need is the conc_queue steal endpoint, already sealed), so no current par target forces sealing it in v1. Sealing is the correct future answer if and when an inline-affine-deque scenario enters the target set; deferring costs nothing measurable now.
falsifiable_check: Preregister before any tuning: (1) Copy-ring microbench — cycle of push_back/pop_front over a 4096-capacity ring of u64 plus a BFS frontier over a 1M-edge graph using the card's ring of u32 handles, whitefoot facts-on vs Rust VecDeque at opt level 3, 30 runs; PASS band: whitefoot geomean within [0.90x, 1.10x] of VecDeque AND the emitted inner loop shows zero residual bounds branches (facts report + disassembly). (2) Affine route — pool-handle ring vs VecDeque<Box<T>> on a 64-byte task type, same band. If (1) falls outside the band or the masked-bound fact fails to elide, CUT-DEFER is wrong and ring must be sealed (or the fact system extended) before v1; if (2) is worse than 1.25x against VecDeque<T> inline (not boxed) on a workload the perf-target set actually contains, escalate the deferred seal.
spec_savings: Removes one whole sealed spec object from v1: roughly 10-14 op rows (push_front/push_back/pop_front/pop_back/front/back/len/capacity/grow/as_slices/make_contiguous/iterate), the wraparound initialized-extent invariant rules (the two-disjoint-live-regions story, which is a second initialization discipline on top of seq's prefix discipline), and the seq-kernel coupling rules for the shared-storage variant — plus the trusted wraparound arithmetic LOC in the kernel (wrap-aware grow memcpy, wrap-aware take/replace), which is exactly the code class (index arithmetic over uninit storage) that historically hides UAF/uninit bugs. Adds: one composition card (Copy masked ring, with the pool-handle variant and the two-seq amortized fallback as subsections) — zero trusted LOC, zero new op rows, one pow2-mask fact idiom that the fact system already covers.
risks: The facts system may not actually prove (i & (cap-1)) < cap from a pow2 prologue fact; if that pattern is outside its proof power, every card-ring access keeps a bounds branch and the Copy route misses par — this is the single load-bearing assumption and is exactly what preregistered check (1) tests.

A genuine inline-affine-T deque hot path (e.g., a parser or scheduler holding owned values by value in a FIFO) may enter the performance-target scenario set later; the pool-handle route concedes ~1 deref + generation check + locality there, and the verdict must flip to sealing if such a scenario must hit par.

Card-quality risk: hand-rolled rings are logic-bug-prone (non-pow2 capacity, full/empty ambiguity at head==tail); the checker keeps them safe but a wrong idiom quietly costs performance, so the card must pin the pow2 + monotone-counter shape normatively.

Deferral creates post-v1 API churn: code written against the card will not automatically migrate to a later sealed ring, and the two spellings (card ring vs sealed ring) would coexist, mildly violating the one-spelling doctrine during transition.
The two-seq fallback for affine T has O(n) worst-case pop latency; if the card oversells it for latency-sensitive loops, users get amortized-only behavior where they expected bounded per-op cost.

user_route: Node = a plain struct stored in a sealed pool<Node> slot: { keys: seq<K, inline B>, vals: seq<V, inline B>, children: seq<handle, inline B+1>, next_leaf: handle }. The floor's critical gap — typed uninitialized affine slots — is exactly what seq<T,inline> already provides sealed: inline capacity, uninit spare, affine elements, take/replace/swap/insert-at/remove-at at the API. So inline affine keys AND values live in the node at std-BTreeMap layout (initialized prefix + len), with zero trust added. Descent: handle deref = base + idx*size + generation check into a contiguous slab (locality equal or better than heap nodes). In-node search: borrow the keys prefix as a slice under a loan token; for Copy K use machine-core SIMD compare + movemask branchless scan (the absl trick); for comparator K, env-struct Ord calls exactly as std does. Rebalancing (the take/replace-across-nodes question): the loan/freeze judgment forbids two simultaneous &uniq node borrows from one pool, so split/merge takes both nodes OUT by value via pool remove (sealed API returns owned Node), drains/appends between the two owned seqs (pure seq API on owned values — no floor violation, these are sealed-container ops plus owned-struct field moves), reinserts, and rewrites the parent's child handles — which rebalancing touches anyway. If pool's link-edit surface already includes a checked disjoint two-slot borrow (split_at_mut analog), even the remove/reinsert copy disappears. Delete returns owned V via seq remove-at. Range scan: leaf-chained cursor follows next_leaf handles, iterating contiguous inline (K,V) prefixes. Bulk-load from sorted: stream-push into leaves left-to-right, build parents level by level, O(n). Ship this as taught checked source (~1-2k LOC), not a composition card — rebalancing is too intricate to ask modest AI writers to rederive from the spec.
perf_argument: Every mechanism the par targets depend on survives the cut. Lookup (~2-3x hash latency): h ≈ log_B(n) dependent loads either way; slab base+idx*size deref is the same latency class as a heap pointer chase and the slab is denser, so cache behavior is at worst equal; the only additions are one predictable generation-check branch per hop (h ≈ 5-7 branches total, ~free) and in-node bounds checks, which the requires-prologue fact system elides since len ≤ inline-capacity is a container invariant (the same fact class that gave the measured 1.71x base64 elision). Branchless in-node scan is fully expressible: portable SIMD compare + movemask + guaranteed-branchless select on Copy keys. Range scan (1-2 ns/element): elements are contiguous inline in leaves, one handle hop per ~B elements, cursor is a loan-typed token with no per-element check after fact elision — this is a pointer-bump loop, on target. Bulk-load: streaming pushes into inline seqs, O(n), no gap. Rebalancing overhead, the one real delta: without a disjoint two-slot borrow, each split/merge pays a pool remove+reinsert (~256B node copy, 2-3 extra writes, one parent-handle rewrite); splits occur O(1/B) per insert, so amortized cost is a few cycles per insert — inside a 1.15x random-insert budget. Fanout: three per-seq len fields vs std's one cost ~8-16B of a 256B node, shaving effective B by ~1; sub-5% depth effect. No missed SIMD, no extra indirection on the hot read paths, no unelidable checks: the route reaches par. Note the earlier objection that killed the naive design — value/key handles into a side pool adding a dependent load per comparison and a cache miss per scanned element — does not apply, because seq<T,inline> puts affine elements inline.
falsifiable_check: Preregister before writing the library: checked-source btree vs Rust std BTreeMap (same machine, same allocator discipline, criterion-style CIs), u64 keys/u64 values at n = 1M uniform-random: (1) point lookup geomean within 1.10x of BTreeMap; (2) random insert within 1.15x; (3) full-range scan ≤ 2.0 ns/element and within 1.15x of BTreeMap's iterator; (4) bulk-load from sorted within 1.10x; plus one affine-payload run (V = 24B string-like box) where scan stays within 1.15x. Bands are two-sided on preregistered seeds; any single metric outside its band after the preregistered tuning budget (node size and fanout only, no protocol changes) falsifies the verdict and reopens KEEP-SEALED. Secondary falsifier: if inspection of optimized bodies shows per-element bounds checks surviving in the scan loop (facts failed to elide), the perf argument's mechanism is wrong regardless of the timing outcome.
spec_savings: Removes the entire btree sealed kernel from the normative surface: roughly 10-14 op rows (get/insert/remove/contains/cursor/range/next-prev/bulk_load/len/clear plus entry-style ops), its cursor-invalidation and rebalancing-atomicity rules, and an estimated 2-3k LOC of trusted rebalancing internals — historically the highest-defect-density container code there is — now shipped as fully checked, zero-trust taught source instead. Net normative add: at most one op row on the already-sealed pool kernel (checked disjoint two-slot &uniq borrow) if its link-edit surface lacks it; even that is optional since the remove/reinsert route needs nothing new. No new language rules.
risks: Load-bearing assumption: seq<T,inline> supports small fixed inline capacities usable as struct fields inside a pool slot, with insert-at/remove-at/drain-range at the API; if inline seq is not embeddable in user structs or lacks range drain, the node representation collapses back to handle-indirection and the par argument fails for affine K/V.

Rebalancing under the loan/freeze judgment relies on pool remove returning the node by value while parent handles are momentarily dangling within one library function; if the checker cannot express this intermediate state without panicky generation-check failures, insert/delete gains extra checked branches on the hot path.

Fact elision of in-node bounds checks is asserted, not yet measured, for loops over a seq prefix reached through a pool deref; if the prologue facts do not flow through the handle deref, range scan carries a per-element check and misses 2 ns/element.
Generation-check branches on descent are assumed predictable; on very deep trees with cold TLB they add measurable latency versus raw-pointer nodes, and no proof currently elides them.

A checked-source library this intricate must still be written correctly by the project; it is zero-trust for soundness but not for availability — a logic bug means wrong ordering results (panics/misses), and the teaching burden (cursors + rebalancing) is the largest of any checked lib proposed so far.

The concurrent ordered map is a v1 non-goal today; if that changes, a lock-free or fine-grained-locked btree is NOT user-buildable (no user atomics) and this unit returns as a sealed candidate — the cut verdict is scoped to the sequential map.

user_route: Per-format layout modules ship as taught checked source following one composition card. (a) Fixed layouts: a module declares chained constant offsets (`const OFF_DST_PORT = OFF_SRC_PORT + 2; ...; const HDR_SIZE = ...`) and one leaf accessor per field, `fn dst_port(b: &[u8]) -> u16 requires b.len >= HDR_SIZE { be16_load(b, OFF_DST_PORT) }`. The caller establishes the size fact once (validate-once); a confined borrow-carrying "validated view" token freezes the buffer while live, so the fact cannot be invalidated by mutation. Every accessor is a trivially-inlined leaf whose constant-offset bounds check is dominated by the prologue fact and elided; the endian 1-mov load supplies the byte order. (b) Data-dependent offsets (TCP options, SQLite cell pointers, TLV): a validation pass walks the structure once, checking `off + width <= len`, and either carries the runtime-offset fact forward or materializes proven offsets into a stack array under the same frozen-buffer token; the card teaches both shapes. (c) Cursor jobs — LEB128/varint, bit readers with 64-bit refill, TLV framing — are checked-library functions built from endian loads, single-instruction bit ops, branchless select, and margin views for checked over-read slack near the buffer end, with amortized `remaining >= K` requires facts. (d) Overlap-tolerant LZ copies: a checked loop that reads a 16-byte chunk from the initialized prefix of a wbuf and stores at the extent, advancing by `min(offset,16)` when offset < 16 — expressible if wbuf permits a read view of the initialized extent concurrent with the append cursor; if the loan judgment forbids that self-alias, add one wbuf member (`copy_within_extend(offset, len)`) rather than keep any compiler-known layout mechanism. No user route exists for a compiler-known schema and none is needed: everything the schema mechanism would generate is exactly this accessor pattern.
perf_argument: Par target is C struct overlay: 1 load + optional bswap per field, zero checks. The checked route hits it mechanically: after inlining, a fixed-field accessor is one movbe/mov+bswap; the bounds check at a compile-time-constant offset is dominated by the single `len >= HDR_SIZE` fact and elided — this is the exact dominated-check pattern the requires-prologue system already demonstrated at 1.71x on base64, and constant-offset domination is its easiest case. On unaligned fields the route is at par or better: the machine-core unaligned endian load is one instruction on x86/ARM, where C struct overlay is UB and portable C falls back to memcpy anyway. A compiler-known schema mechanism generates the same loads from the same facts — it cannot beat 1 load — so its only possible wins are ergonomic, not performance. For data-dependent offsets, C also hand-rolls (a static schema doesn't cover them either), so the par comparison is C's checked-or-trusting cursor code; the whitefoot route either carries the runtime fact (zero residual cost) or, worst case, keeps one predicted-never-taken branch per access, and the validation-pass-plus-proven-offset-array shape removes even that for hot page walks. Varint/bit-reader loops are load/shift/mask/select sequences with margin-view over-read slack, instruction-identical to the C idiom. LZ copy loops match zlib/miniz chunked-copy codegen provided the wbuf self-alias read+append is admitted; that is the single mechanism gap, and it is an API-shape question, not a missing instruction.
falsifiable_check: Preregister four checked-source implementations per the card: (1) IPv4+TCP fixed-header accessors, (2) SQLite b-tree page cell-pointer walk (data-dependent offsets), (3) protobuf-style varint decode, (4) an LZ back-reference copy loop over wbuf. Gate 1 (codegen inspection): every fixed-field accessor after the validate fact must compile to exactly 1 load plus at most 1 bswap/movbe — zero residual bounds checks, zero branches; any surviving check falsifies the verdict. Gate 2 (measured): geomean throughput vs pinned C references (hand-written struct-overlay packet parser, SQLite's own cell walk shape, protobuf C varint, miniz inflate copy loop) must be >= 0.97x with CI excluding 0.95x; below that band, or if the wbuf self-alias LZ loop is unwritable without new trusted surface larger than one member, the verdict is wrong and the schema half re-adjudicates toward KEEP-SEALED.
spec_savings: Removes the entire compiler-known layout-schema mechanism from the normative surface: schema declaration grammar (field/endian/offset/align rows), schema well-formedness rules, the schema-to-fact and schema-to-access lowering rules, and their trusted lowering LOC — a trusted surface where a codegen bug is silent out-of-bounds access. Adds: one taught composition card (validated-view layout module pattern: chained const offsets, leaf accessors, one requires clause, validation-pass shape for variable layouts), shipped checked library modules with zero trust, and at most one new wbuf API member for overlapping extend-copy if the loan judgment cannot already express it. Net: several spec rule-rows and a trusted generator deleted in exchange for one card and zero-trust source.
risks: Data-dependent offset facts: if the requires system handles only compile-time-constant bounds and cannot carry a runtime `off + w <= len` fact (or an element-wise fact over a validated-offset array) across the access site, variable-layout hot loops (DB pages, TLV) keep one branch per access; likely within the 3% band but must be measured, and scalar-only fact language would make the proven-offset-array shape ineffective.

Loan/freeze may forbid reading the initialized prefix of a wbuf while an append cursor is live, blocking overlap-tolerant LZ copies in pure checked source; fallback is one new wbuf member (copy_within_extend), which is small sealed-surface growth that must itself pass hostile review.

Writer-correctness bug class: hand-chained const offsets can be wrong without any safety violation (reading the wrong field type-checks); the schema mechanism would have caught width/offset drift structurally. Mitigation is card discipline (each offset defined as previous + previous-width, final size assertion) plus format round-trip tests, but the checker cannot enforce it.
Accessor inlining is load-bearing: if the compiler ever fails to inline trivial leaf accessors, per-field cost becomes call + load and par is lost; the codegen-inspection gate must pin this.

Validate-once soundness assumes the frozen-buffer token actually excludes mutation; for mmap'd files written by another process the fact is void — same hazard as C, but the card must explicitly scope validate-once claims to private/owned mappings so writers do not overclaim.

ISA variance: on targets without cheap unaligned loads the endian 1-mov claim degrades to byte assembly; par vs portable C is preserved (C has the same problem) but absolute numbers on such targets will miss the band and the preregistered benchmark should pin x86-64 and AArch64 explicitly.

user_route: (a) Bytescan/hash kernels: already planned as checked source and correctly so. memchr/memmem/utf8-validate/json-stage1/base64 are wide loads + compares + movemask + branchless select over byte data: machine core gives the SIMD value types, named intrinsics, one-time ISA dispatch, and endian loads; wbuf margin views give the overlapping final-vector load that memchr-class kernels use for tails; requires-prologue facts elide the dominated `i + VLEN <= len` bounds in the bulk loop. crc32c uses the named CRC intrinsic; xxh3/siphash are pure mul/rot/xor on Copy integers. Zero sealing needed. (b) Alg kit: unseal the sort core. The par target (ipnsort-class on random u64) lives entirely in the Copy-key domain, and for Copy T every unsafe trick in ipnsort has a checked equivalent: the insertion-sort "hole" becomes copy-to-local + overwrite + write-back (overwriting a Copy slice slot is plain checked assignment, identical codegen to the move-out-of-hole version); the branchless block partition writes u8 offsets into a wbuf, then swaps/copies at `base + off` where `off <= 255` is a type-level fact and `base + 256 <= len` is a loop-dominated requires fact, so bounds elide; heapsort fallback and 4-ary heap are pure index-swap loops with `child < len` guards that double as the algorithmic bound. Radix auto-dispatch on closed derived keys is pure checked code (histogram + scatter into a second fully-initialized Copy buffer). Large/affine T sorts ship as a taught composition card: sort Copy (key, u32 idx) pairs with the checked core, then apply the permutation by cycle-following take/replace over the sealed seq<T> API — which is also the fast route in C++/Rust for large elements, so the card is not a downgrade. Branchless binary search: guaranteed-branchless select + `lo + half < len` facts, checked. itoa/ryu: table-driven Copy integer arithmetic, checked trivially. The one thing users genuinely cannot build is a par STABLE sort of affine T (driftsort merges need typed uninitialized affine scratch, which the floor forbids and wbuf's byte-only rule cannot fake) — that capability is not in this unit's par target and is deferred, not sealed today.
perf_argument: The 55-80M u64/s ipnsort figure is set by the partition loop: per element, one vector-or-scalar compare, one predicated u8-offset store, and one gather-style swap — all branch-free. In the checked route every instruction survives intact: compares and offset stores are machine-core ops on a wbuf (byte-typed, so no affine machinery); the element moves are Copy assignments, which lower to the same mov/vmovdqu as Rust's ptr::copy_nonoverlapping for u64; the only candidate overhead is bounds checks, and each hot-loop index is either dominated by a prologue fact (block base) or bounded by its type (u8 offset < block size 256), so the measured fact-elision machinery (1.71x recovered on base64; percent-encoding beaten 1.653x even with ALL bounds retained per D9a) removes them or demonstrably tolerates them. Insertion-sort base case for Copy keys uses copy-out/shift-by-assignment/copy-in — the same n+2 moves as the unsafe hole version, not 3n swap moves, because Copy overwrite is legal; the 3x-move penalty applies only to affine T, which is off the par path via the sort-indices card. Bytescan kernels are pure bandwidth: memchr-crate's ~2.7GB/s/lane comes from 32-64B loads + PMOVMSKB + tzcnt, every one of which is a named machine-core op, and the margin-view tail keeps the last iteration in vector form. Hash kernels have no bounds-dense inner structure at all (fixed-stride Copy loads via endian 1-mov). The sealed sort core therefore buys zero mechanism the floor lacks for the named target; sealing it would be trust spent on convenience, which D16 forbids.
falsifiable_check: Preregister before writing code: port ipnsort's core (block partition + Copy insertion base + heapsort fallback, radix dispatch off) as fully checked whitefoot; benchmark random u64, N = 10^6, same host and toolchain as the Rust ipnsort reference, 30 runs, bootstrap CI. Pass band: checked whitefoot >= 0.90x of measured ipnsort throughput AND the optimizer's facts report + optimized instruction bodies show the partition inner loop free of residual bounds branches (or, if bounds are retained, throughput still in band, matching the D9a retained-bounds precedent). Secondary: checked memchr on 1MiB haystacks >= 0.90x of memchr-crate per lane. If either lands below 0.90x with the fact report confirming the promised elisions fired, the CUT verdict is wrong and the sort core (or the failing kernel) returns to sealed.
spec_savings: Removes the sealed-sort-core op row and its trusted internals (~1-2k LOC of partition/merge/hole code that would otherwise need hostile adversarial review as a fact-adjacent trusted surface) from the normative catalog; the entire unit becomes zero-trust checked source. Adds: one composition card (sort-large-or-affine-by-key-then-permute via seq take/replace cycle-following) and one spec clarification that integer-typed values carry their type-range as a usable fact (u8 index < 256) — a rule the fact system likely needs anyway. Net: minus one sealed component, minus one standing review obligation, plus one card, zero new op rows. No members trimmed from the assumed-kept sealed kernels; the card leans on seq's existing take/replace/swap rows.
risks: Stable par-performance sort of affine T remains unimplementable from the floor (needs typed uninit affine scratch for merges); if a real workload demands it, a narrow sealed stable-merge core must be admitted later — this verdict defers, it does not solve.

The bounds-elision argument for the block partition assumes type-range facts (u8 < 256) are expressible in the requires system; if not, the hottest loop keeps a data-dependent bounds check per element, and partition is more bounds-dense than the D9a workloads that tolerated retained checks.

The large-T composition card's performance rests on seq take/replace being O(1) with negligible occupancy-tracking overhead inside the sealed kernel; if take/replace carries per-call state churn, cycle-following permutation misses par for large elements.

Affine-T in-place sorting via swap-only paths eats ~2-3x move traffic in insertion-sort runs versus Rust's hole technique; acceptable only because the par target is Copy-key, but it should be documented as a known checked-route gap, not hidden.

Bytescan tails at full speed require inputs held in wbuf with margin views; kernels invoked on plain slices fall back to scalar tails, penalizing short-input latency benchmarks (memchr on <64B strings) even though bulk throughput is unaffected.

json-stage1 (simdjson-style structural indexing) is the most intricate kernel in the set; if its clmul/pdep-style dependency chain needs an intrinsic missing from machine core's named list, the fix is adding the intrinsic row to machine core, not sealing the kernel — but that is still a spec addition to budget.

unit: shared-rcu (shared<T> epoch-pinned publish/snapshot + rcu_table<K,V> papaya-class concurrent read-mostly table)
user_route: Keep shared<T> sealed — users cannot build it: zero-shared-line snapshots require per-thread epoch slots, acquire/release fences, and grace-period reclamation, all of which need raw atomics and memory-model reasoning the floor forbids; the only floor-reachable substitute is a refcounted handle behind a mutex or atomic cell, and the refcount write is exactly the shared-line traffic that kills the M7 scaling target. Trim rcu_table<K,V> to two taught composition cards. Card 1 (read-mostly, the arc-swap pattern): shared<table<K,V>> + a writer mutex<pending_batch>. Readers: snapshot() -> confined borrow of the inner sealed table -> table.get(); the loan/freeze judgment already typechecks the snapshot guard. Writers: lock, accumulate updates into a small ordinary table, every T ms (or every k updates) clone the current table (iterate+insert into a fresh table; requires K,V explicit-copy or shared-boxed V), apply the batch, publish, unlock. Multi-writer read-modify-write is serialized by the mutex, so no compare-and-publish is needed for correctness. Card 2 (mixed loads, sharded-mutex-map): [cachepad mutex<table<K,V>>; S] with shard = high bits of the existing hash; pure floor composition over sealed mutex, sealed table, monomorphized generics. A papaya-class in-place concurrent table is NOT user-buildable from the floor (needs per-bucket CAS, hazard/epoch-guarded incremental migration, take/replace of affine values out of a user container — all excluded), so it is deferred on the standard escalation lane, not declared user-implementable.
perf_argument: Read par (>=12x at 16 cores, Zipf 99.9% reads): the card meets or beats papaya. Reader cost = one uncontended per-thread epoch-pin store (~1-2ns) + a plain sealed SwissTable probe (SIMD group match, no atomic entry loads, no migration-in-progress branches). Papaya's read is the same pin plus a probe that must use atomic meta loads and tolerate concurrent migration. Neither reader writes a shared line, so both scale linearly; per-op the card's frozen-snapshot probe is <= papaya's. Read scaling is a property of shared<T>, which we keep sealed — the card inherits it for free. Update par (papaya in-place, ~100-300ns/update): the card does NOT reach it, mechanically and irreducibly — publish is an O(n) rebuild. Per-update publish caps at u ~ 1/(n*c_copy): with ~50-100ns per rebuilt entry, n=10^6 gives ~10-20ms per publish, i.e. ~50-100 sustained updates/s. Batching k updates per publish amortizes to n*c/k per update and one writer core sustains ~10^4-10^5 updates/s at n=10^6 with ~10ms visibility staleness and a transient 2x memory footprint; visibility latency and staleness are the price, and they grow with n. The break point is u*n ≳ 10^7-10^8 entries/s or any sub-ms-visibility requirement — beyond that only in-place update wins, and the sharded-mutex card covers throughput there but degrades read scaling under Zipf hot-shard lock convoy (readers now write lock lines; expect ~6-10x, below M7). So the card covers the actual read-mostly regime (config/routing/feature-flag tables update at Hz-to-kHz, well inside the ceiling); the high-churn concurrent-cache regime (>10^5 in-place updates/s with concurrent linear read scaling) is genuinely uncovered and is the deferral, not a claimed equivalence. None of the fourteen frozen corpus operations (Hashbrown/mimalloc/SQLite/Crossbeam) requires that regime, and concurrent ordered map is already a non-goal — the concurrent unordered table's high-churn slice is the same class of deferrable.
falsifiable_check: Preregister before implementation: build both cards from the floor exactly as taught (no private intrinsics), then run the M7 harness at 16 cores, Zipf-skewed 99.9% read / 0.1% update, n=10^6 entries, against a pinned papaya reference (papaya 0.2.x, same key/value shape). Bands that falsify TRIM-MEMBERS: (a) COW card read scaling < 12x vs its own 1-core baseline, or per-read latency > 1.3x papaya's read at 16 cores; (b) COW card cannot sustain >= 10^4 updates/s at n=10^6 with <= 10ms visibility staleness and read scaling still >= 12x; (c) sharded-mutex card at 99% read / 1% write delivers < 0.5x papaya aggregate throughput at 16 cores. Any one band failing flips rcu_table back to KEEP-SEALED. Additionally falsified by workload evidence: if a declared v1 target scenario is shown to require > 10^5 in-place updates/s with concurrent >= 12x read scaling and < 1ms visibility, the deferral is wrong and rcu_table seals in v1.
spec_savings: Removes the largest remaining concurrent-container surface: rcu_table's op rows (get/insert/update/remove/contains/len/iter plus resize, iteration-consistency, and update-visibility wording — roughly 10-15 rows and the hardest linearizability prose in the catalog) and its trusted internals (a papaya-class table is thousands of trusted LOC: per-bucket CAS protocol, incremental migration state machine, EBR integration — every line a fact-channel-adjacent soundness liability requiring hostile review). Keeps shared<T> at ~5 op rows (new/publish/snapshot/guard-drop, optionally update-with) with one crisp rule: readers write zero shared lines, reclamation after grace period. Adds two composition cards (COW-republish with staleness/memory caveats stated on the card; sharded-mutex-map), zero trusted LOC. Net: minus one sealed component and its concurrency proofs, plus two zero-trust cards.
risks: A v1 workload in the high-churn regime (concurrent cache/session store, >10^5 updates/s with concurrent reads) surfaces late; the escalation lane then has to seal rcu_table under time pressure, and a papaya-class table is among the hardest units to review hostilely.

Staleness misuse by modest AI writers: the COW card's snapshot-read-then-publish window invites lost read-modify-write updates unless the card mandates routing all mutations through the writer mutex; the card text must make the mutex non-optional or teach shared<T>.update-with.

Affine values: rebuilding the table requires K,V explicit-copy or shared-boxed values; workloads with large non-copy V pay an extra indirection on the read path (one more pointer chase, ~5-15ns), which could shave the read-latency band even though scaling holds.
Transient 2x table footprint during republish; at large n this can breach memory budgets that papaya's in-place resize would not.

Zipf hot-shard lock convoy on the sharded-mutex card means the mixed-load fallback misses the 12x read-scaling band; if mixed loads turn out to be the common case rather than the rare one, the two-card coverage story collapses.
shared<T> as specified must expose a cheap update-with or the multi-writer story leans entirely on the external mutex; that is correct but must be stated on the card, not discovered by the writer.

The M7 falsification bands are set from mechanism estimates (entry copy cost, pin cost) rather than measured whitefoot numbers; if the sealed table's clone path is slower than the ~50-100ns/entry assumption, the update-rate ceiling drops below the band and the verdict flips on (b).

user_route: Ship `wal` as taught checked source over three kept sealed forms: file, sync, and (optionally) evring. Concrete construction: (1) Log frames live in an align(4096) wbuf — header {payload_len, seqno, crc32c} + payload, built with checked partial byte initialization and NT copy; O_DIRECT-compatible alignment comes from wbuf's align(N), and crc32c comes from machine-core named intrinsics (software slice-by-8 fallback is fine, see perf). (2) Group commit is the classic leader-follower pattern over mutex<WalState> + condvar from the sealed sync vocabulary: committers append their frames into the shared wbuf under the mutex; the first waiter becomes leader, issues one appending pwrite + one fdatasync via file leaf rows for the whole batch, then broadcasts; followers just wait on the condvar. No user atomics or memory-model reasoning is needed — the mutex/condvar are the entire concurrency surface. (3) Checkpoint/atomic-replace is write-new-file, fdatasync(file), rename(tmp, live), fsync(parent dir) — all file leaf ops. (4) Recovery is a sequential read scan validating per-frame checksums, truncating at the first invalid frame (checksums make torn tail writes self-detecting, so no sector-size oracle is required, matching SQLite's discipline). (5) An optional fast path submits write→fdatasync as linked completions on evring if evring's rows expose ordering links. PRECONDITION (this is the one seam): the sealed file form must expose fdatasync, rename, fsync-on-directory-handle, and an O_DIRECT/alignment-honoring open+pwrite row as leaf operations. If directory fsync or fdatasync were withheld from file, the card cannot deliver rename durability on Linux and the verdict flips to TRIM-MEMBERS (keep a minimal sealed durability kernel). With those rows present, every remaining line of WAL logic — framing, batching, checksums, checkpoint scheduling, recovery — is ordinary checked whitefoot with zero trusted internals.
perf_argument: The par target (SQLite-class group-commit durability) is fdatasync-bound, not CPU-bound, so sealing buys nothing mechanically. Per-commit user-space work is: memcpy of the record into the log buffer (wbuf + NT copy runs at memory bandwidth, identical to what sealed code would emit), a crc32c over the payload (hardware crc32c ~20+ GB/s; even software slice-by-8 at ~1-3 GB/s is <1 microsecond for typical sub-kilobyte commit records), and one mutex acquire + condvar wait (~1-2 microseconds under contention with a parking-lot-class sealed mutex). The batch then pays one pwrite + one fdatasync: 50-500 microseconds on NVMe, milliseconds on SATA. The syscall dominates by 2-3 orders of magnitude, and the card issues exactly the same syscall sequence SQLite's C WAL issues — same count, same ordering (log write, log fdatasync, and for checkpoint: new-file fdatasync, rename, dir fsync). There is no mechanism a sealed implementation could reach that the card cannot: no private atomics needed (leader-follower needs only mutex/condvar and is the standard design in SQLite/Postgres group commit), no kernel bypass (fsync chaining via io_uring linked SQEs is reachable through kept-sealed evring), no allocation on the commit path (wbuf is preallocated), and requires-prologue facts elide the bounds checks in the frame-serialization loop. Scaling under N concurrent committers matches the reference because batching collapses N fsyncs into 1 regardless of who wrote the batching loop. The only residual gaps are second-order: condvar broadcast wakeup storms at very high committer counts (also present in the C references) and software-CRC bandwidth during recovery scans (rare path). Neither touches the steady-state commit throughput target.
falsifiable_check: Preregister before writing the lib: implement the checked-source group-commit WAL over file+sync per the route above; benchmark commit throughput (txns/sec, fixed 256B and 4KB payloads) at 1, 4, and 16 concurrent committers on NVMe, in both fdatasync-per-batch (FULL) and no-fsync-per-commit (NORMAL) disciplines, against SQLite 3.4x in WAL mode on the same hardware performing equivalent single-table inserts, and against a strace/bpftrace syscall-count trace of both. Bands that falsify CUT-TO-CHECKED-LIB: (a) checked-lib throughput below 0.90x of SQLite WAL at any concurrency point after normalizing for SQLite's B-tree work (or below 0.95x of a minimal C group-commit harness doing only log appends, which is the cleaner apples-to-apples reference); (b) syscall count per committed batch exceeding the C reference's; (c) a crash-consistency run (dm-log-writes or fsync-fault injection, 10k random-kill trials) losing or corrupting any acknowledged transaction — that would indicate a missing file leaf row (dir-fsync/fdatasync semantics) that only a sealed form could provide. Any one of (a)-(c) flips the verdict.
spec_savings: Removes the entire wal sealed surface: roughly 6-10 op rows (open-wal, append/commit, group-commit config knobs, checkpoint, atomic-replace, recovery-open) plus its trusted implementation — group-commit concurrency, recovery scanning, and checkpoint scheduling, realistically 1-3k trusted LOC that would otherwise need hostile review as a fact-adjacent durability kernel. Adds: 2-3 leaf rows to the kept file form (fdatasync — already listed as an escape, fsync-on-directory, rename; plus an alignment note on the O_DIRECT open row), one taught composition card (durability ordering discipline: what to fsync, in what order, and why), and one checked-source library that is fully user-visible and carries zero trust. Net: the io family sheds its largest non-syscall member; the normative spec keeps only true syscall leaves in file, and durability policy moves entirely to teachable, reviewable checked code.
risks: file's leaf rows are load-bearing: if fdatasync, rename, or directory-handle fsync is not exposed as a file op, the card cannot achieve rename durability on Linux and this verdict silently fails closed to TRIM-MEMBERS — verify the file row set before ratifying.

Durability ordering is semantically unverifiable by the checker (it proves memory safety, not fsync ordering); a user who forks or hand-rolls the pattern instead of using the shipped checked lib can write a memory-safe WAL that loses data on crash. Mitigation is the teaching card plus shipping the lib as the blessed writing, but the failure mode exists and is invisible until a crash.

condvar-based leader-follower may lag a futex-tuned native group commit under extreme committer counts (hundreds of threads) if the sealed sync mutex/condvar is not parking-lot-class; this is a sync-vocabulary quality dependency, not a wal-sealing argument.

If machine core lacks a crc32c named intrinsic, recovery-scan and large-payload checksum bandwidth drops to software-CRC rates (~1-3 GB/s); steady-state commit throughput is unaffected but recovery time on multi-GB logs degrades measurably.

The evring fast path (linked write→fdatasync submissions) assumes evring exposes ordering links in its op rows; if it does not, the card is limited to synchronous file syscalls — still at SQLite par, but below what a liburing-native WAL could do for high-QD workloads.

O_DIRECT correctness requires runtime knowledge of the device's logical block size for alignment; wbuf align(4096) covers the common case, but 512e/4Kn edge devices may need an os/file metadata query row that does not currently exist in the floor.

user_route: No member of this unit is user-implementable at all — the floor exposes no atomic type, no memory-model vocabulary, and no thread spawning, so everything kept stays sealed. The trim question is therefore about which sealed members earn a row. Keep: relaxed counter, publish/snapshot cell, futex-adaptive mutex<T> with loan-typed guard, once<T>, condvar with consume-and-reissue guard, and conc_queue spsc + mpmc endpoints. Cut cas-cell: every scenario nominated for a raw CAS cell maps onto a kept form — fetch-add/statistics/epoch and id allocation → relaxed counter; publish-new-immutable-state / read-mostly config swap → publish/snapshot cell; guarded mutation of arbitrary T → mutex<T>; one-time initialization → once<T>; ownership handoff → queue endpoints; freelist/probe/slab CAS protocols → already internal to the sealed pool/table/arena/seq kernels. The residual raw-CAS use cases (Treiber stacks, seqlocks, tagged-pointer tricks, custom lock-free containers) are unbuildable on this floor anyway — users lack typed uninit slots, take/replace out of user containers, and any second atomic to complete a protocol — so a CAS on a lone Copy scalar cell synchronizes with nothing the user can express; the member serves no completable user program. Demote steal from user endpoint to internal detail of par/threads: the work-stealing deque stays in the sealed scheduler, and the only user-visible consumers of steal (hand-rolled schedulers, actor runtimes) route through par parallel-for / scoped spawn for fork-join and mpmc for irregular task graphs.
perf_argument: The keeps carry the par targets: futex-adaptive mutex is the parking_lot/std-mutex mechanism (inline uncontended CAS, adaptive spin, futex park) and the loan-typed guard is zero-cost; publish/snapshot cell's read path is a single load-acquire, matching arc-swap/RCU-read state of the art for read-mostly sharing — mutex cannot substitute (contended CAS per read); relaxed counter compiles to lock xadd / plain add, matching C++ relaxed atomics; once<T> is the one-time-dispatch mechanism the machine-core ISA dispatch already depends on; spsc/mpmc are the crossbeam-channel-class mechanisms. The cuts cost nothing measurable for v1: all fourteen frozen Hashbrown/mimalloc/SQLite/Crossbeam operations execute their CAS protocols inside sealed kernels, so cas-cell removal changes no hot path; the Chase-Lev deque still runs at crossbeam-deque parity inside par — demoting steal changes visibility, not code. The only mechanism gap opened is user-authored schedulers replacing steal with mpmc, paying roughly one extra contended CAS per pop and losing owner-side LIFO locality under load — accepted as outside v1 scenarios, since users cannot spawn the threads such schedulers need anyway except through the same sealed par forms.
falsifiable_check: Preregister two experiments before any reinstatement debate. (1) cas-cell necessity sweep: enumerate the fourteen frozen operations plus a fixed, pre-nominated set of 10 v1 concurrency scenario kernels (config hot-swap, stats aggregation, lazy global init, pipeline, fan-in/fan-out, shutdown signaling, bounded producer/consumer, read-mostly cache, id allocation, worker pool); implement each from the trimmed vocabulary only; verdict is falsified if any scenario cannot reach within 1.10x median throughput of its named C/C++/Rust reference (arc-swap, parking_lot, crossbeam-channel, std::sync::Once, C11-atomics baselines) without writing a raw CAS retry loop. (2) steal demotion check: irregular fork-join workload (parallel quicksort or unbalanced task tree, 8 cores) implemented via par parallel-for versus a crossbeam-deque hand-rolled scheduler reference; band: the par route must land within 1.10x of the crossbeam-deque reference; if it falls outside the band and a user-facing steal endpoint demonstrably closes the gap, reinstate steal as an endpoint.
spec_savings: Removes the cas-cell op row and, with it, the entire compare-exchange teaching obligation — retry-loop discipline, ABA discussion, and the last pressure to ever document an ordering menu (the sync page keeps exactly one rule per member: counter = no publication, cell = snapshot sees fully-built value, mutex/condvar = guard discipline, once = first-wins). Removes the steal endpoint row from conc_queue (3 endpoints → 2); the deque implementation remains as already-trusted LOC inside the par kernel, so zero trusted lines are added and none of the remaining rows gain caveats. Net: two op rows and the memory-model teaching appendix deleted from the normative surface; no additions.
risks: A legitimate v1 scenario may need version-stamped optimistic reads (seqlock-style high-rate telemetry) that publish/snapshot cell cannot express; mitigation is additive — a sealed seqlock-cell can be added later without breaking anything, whereas un-exposing cas-cell later would break users.

User-authored schedulers or actor runtimes on mpmc underperform a steal deque under contention (extra contended CAS per pop, lost LIFO locality); if such runtimes become v1-relevant the steal demotion must be revisited via the preregistered check.

Without cas-cell, writers may abuse relaxed counter as a done-flag assuming publication ordering it does not provide; the counter's one-line rule must state 'no publication' explicitly or this becomes a silent-misuse channel.

condvar is kept on classical-pair grounds but is the weakest keep: if the queue endpoints turn out to cover all v1 blocking-wait patterns, it is itself speculative surface that a later trim pass should re-examine.

Deferral of cas-cell does not delete its teaching cost, it postpones it: any future reinstatement pulls the full memory-model burden back in, so reinstatement must clear the same D16 bar, not ride in as a convenience.

user_route: Core keeps are confirmed non-implementable, each with a named blocking mechanism at the floor. seq: growable storage for affine T requires typed uninitialized spare-capacity slots plus take/replace out of the container; wbuf is bytes-only, so the only user routes are Option-tagged slots (per-element tag byte + branch on every access, breaks flat memcpy growth) or default-init on reserve (O(n) writes per grow) — neither is par with Vec. table: the control-byte array IS user-buildable (wbuf + machine-core SIMD group probes), but the K/V slot array is uninit affine storage with take/replace on probe hit — same blocker; a user SwissTable cannot exist. pool: generational slab needs reusable uninit typed slots + generation-checked take; a user version over seq must keep slots always-initialized (tombstone Option), paying tag+branch per access vs slab/slotmap. arena kernel: typed emplacement of affine values into raw region memory is exactly the forbidden operation; bump-alloc cannot be recreated over wbuf for non-byte T. threads+par, sync, conc_queue: no user spawn and no exposed atomic type — categorically closed. extern-C records: C ABI layout control is a codegen guarantee, not expressible in checked source. Language primitives (loan/freeze, wbuf/margin, machine core, brands, copy struct) are checker/codegen-level and definitionally not library-implementable. The trims: (a) table's inline=N tiny-map knob → users reach par via a composition card: linear-scan small-map over seq<(K,V)> with machine-core SIMD key compare, which matches or beats hashing for n≲8 (the entire point of tiny-map modes); (b) seq's inline (SmallVec) knob → no exact user route exists for affine T (same uninit-slot blocker), but the near route — arena-backed seq with capacity hint, amortizing the allocation the inline buffer exists to avoid — covers most tiny-vector call sites; defer the knob behind a bench trigger rather than carry dual-representation trusted code from day one. Missing-irreducible check: priority queue = sift over seq using seq's API swap (fine, facts elide the dominated bounds checks); graphs ride pool (fine); interning rides table+seq (fine); FIFO/deque is the one silent assumption — no user route to VecDeque's contiguous ring (uninit affine slots again); cover v1 with a two-stack FIFO card (two seqs, amortized O(1), each element moved twice) and defer a sealed deque.
perf_argument: Every retained kernel's par target fails mechanically without sealing: vs Vec, Option-tagged or default-init storage adds either a branch+tag on the hottest load/store path or O(n) initialization per reserve — both visible in any push-heavy loop and not elidable by the fact system (the tag is semantic, not a bounds check). vs hashbrown, losing take/replace on probe forces value copies or tombstones in the slot array, breaking the one-load-one-compare hit path. vs slab/slotmap and bumpalo/typed_arena, freelist-with-generations and pointer-bump alloc (2-3 instructions) both require typed reuse of dead storage. pool does NOT subsume arena: pool alloc = freelist pop + generation write + key construction vs arena's bump — roughly 3-5x the instruction count on the alloc fast path, and arena's O(1) bulk free vs pool's per-element accounting; keep both. For the trims: tiny-map linear scan with SIMD compare is the state-of-the-art mechanism for n≤8 (it is what inline map modes compile down to), so the card concedes nothing; the seq-inline defer concedes only allocation-avoidance constant factors on workloads dominated by many short-lived tiny vectors (compiler/AST-shaped code), which the frozen fourteen-operation corpus does not center, and arena-backing recovers most of that constant. Two-stack FIFO concedes a 2x move constant per element against VecDeque, but moves are memcpy-cheap and BFS/queue workloads are typically dominated by the per-element work, not the queue itself.
falsifiable_check: Preregister three benches before v1 freeze. (1) Tiny-map: hashbrown-style lookup/insert workload with map sizes drawn n∈[1,8] — the linear-scan-card map must be within 1.05x of an inline-mode SwissTable reference; worse re-admits table's inline=N. (2) Tiny-vector: a token/AST-shaped workload (many vectors, median len≤4) — arena-backed seq must be within 1.10x end-to-end of a SmallVec reference; worse re-admits seq's inline knob. (3) FIFO: BFS over a 10M-edge graph — two-stack-FIFO card must be within 1.15x of a VecDeque reference; worse admits a sealed deque kernel. Any single band failure falsifies the corresponding trim; all three passing plus no corpus operation requiring double-ended or sorted-map access confirms the verdict.
spec_savings: Removes: the inline=N type parameter and its dual-representation semantics from table (op rows for the inline mode, spill/promotion rules, and the branchy small-mode trusted internals — the largest single trusted-LOC pocket in the map kernel) and the inline knob from seq (same class: dual storage representation, promotion rule, several hundred trusted LOC of the least-exercised paths in the kernel). Adds: two composition cards, zero trust — small-map linear-scan-over-seq and two-stack FIFO — plus one deferred-decision note (sealed deque, sealed sorted map) gated on the preregistered benches. Net: two fewer type-parameter rows and two fewer dual-representation rule clusters in the normative surface, in exchange for two documented patterns; every sealed kernel that remains carries a one-line non-forgeability justification (uninit affine slots / take-replace / atomics / spawn / ABI / intrinsics).
risks: Deque gap: the two-stack card covers FIFO only; a genuinely double-ended hot loop (sliding-window, work-stealing-adjacent local deques outside conc_queue) has no par route and would force a mid-cycle kernel admission.

Sorted-map gap: B-tree nodes need uninit affine slot arrays, so no user BTreeMap at par exists; if a corpus-adjacent workload needs ordered iteration + point updates, the fallback (sorted seq, O(n) insert) fails at scale and a sealed btree becomes a forced late add.
seq-inline defer bets against compiler/AST-shaped workloads; if whitefoot's own self-hosted xlc is SmallVec-hungry, the 1.10x band will fail on the project's most visible program.

User-written sort/heap at par depends on the requires-prologue facts actually eliding swap-path bounds checks through seq's API swap; this is asserted from the base64 result, not yet measured on a permutation kernel.

Tiny-map card correctness burden shifts to taught source: a modest-capability writer must get the SIMD compare card right from the spec alone; a wrong-but-checked linear scan is safe but silently below par, which the gate would not catch.

Trim assumes re-admission of a knob later is purely additive; if seq's ABI (three-word header) gets frozen into extern boundaries or serialized layouts before the bench trigger fires, adding inline storage later becomes a breaking layout change.
